narration: Define polymorphism as the ability of a method or object to react in many different ways.
Explain that the term polymorphism is derived from 'poly' meaning many and 'morphism' meaning behavior.
Illustrate polymorphism with a real-life analogy where one person behaves differently at school versus at home.
Categorize polymorphism into two types: compile-time polymorphism and runtime polymorphism.
Define compile-time polymorphism as a scenario where the result of a method is known before execution.
Define runtime polymorphism as a scenario where the specific method to be executed is determined during execution.
Identify method overloading as the implementation of compile-time polymorphism.
Identify method overriding as the implementation of runtime polymorphism.
Create a class named Addition to demonstrate method overloading.
Define a method named add that takes two integer arguments (n1, n2) and returns their sum.
Overload the add method by creating a version that takes three integer arguments.
Overload the add method again by creating a version that takes four double arguments.
Instantiate an object of the Addition class.
Call the first add method with two integer arguments (10, 2) and store the result in a variable.
Call the second add method with three integer arguments (5, 6, 5) and store the result in a variable.
Call the third add method with four double arguments (8, 9, 2, 3) and store the result in a variable.
Print the results of the three overloaded methods to the console.
Create a parent class named ClassA containing a void method named show.
Implement the show method in ClassA to print 'This is A'.
Create a child class named ClassB that extends ClassA.
Override the show method in ClassB to print 'This is B'.
Instantiate an object of the child class ClassB.
Call the show method on the ClassB object to demonstrate runtime polymorphism.
Explain that the child class method overrides the parent class method at runtime.
Demonstrate how to access the parent class method using the 'super' keyword within the child class.
Summarize that method overloading occurs at compile time while method overriding occurs at runtime.
